title: षोडश गणपति स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री षोडश गणपति स्तोत्रम् ॥

ॐ बालगणपतये नमः।
करस्थकदलीचूतपनसेक्षुकमोदकम्।
बालसूर्यप्रभं वन्दे देवं बालगणाधिपम्॥१॥

ॐ तरुणगणपतये नमः।
पाशाङ्कुशापूपमोदकैः कपित्थजम्बूफलैर्युतम्।
बालार्कसदृशच्छायं तरुणं गणनायकम्॥२॥

ॐ भक्तगणपतये नमः।
नारिकेलाम्रकदलीगुडपायसधारिणम्।
शरच्चन्द्राभवपुषं भजे भक्तगणाधिपम्॥३॥

ॐ वीरगणपतये नमः।
बेताललूलितभुजं शक्तिशूलाङ्कुशोज्ज्वलम्।
खड्गचक्रगदापाणिं वीरं विघ्नेश्वरं भजे॥४॥

ॐ शक्तिगणपतये नमः।
आलिङ्ग्य देवीमभितो निषण्णां
परस्परासक्तकटीनिवेशम्।
सन्ध्यारुणं पाशसृणीवहन्तं
भयापहं शक्तिगणेशमीडे॥५॥

ॐ द्विजगणपतये नमः।
यज्ञोपवीतं दधतं दण्डकाक्षमणीगुणम्।
पुस्तकं चोत्तमाम्भोजं द्विजरूपं गणाधिपम्॥६॥

ॐ सिद्धिगणपतये नमः।
पक्वचूतफलं तिलमोदकं च सुसंयुतम्।
इक्षुदण्डं च पुष्पं च दधतं सिद्धिनायकम्॥७॥

ॐ उच्छिष्टगणपतये नमः।
नीलाब्जदाडिमीवीणापूर्णपात्रधरं प्रभुम्।
ललन्तिकासमायुक्तमुच्छिष्टगणनायकम्॥८॥

ॐ विघ्नगणपतये नमः।
पाशाङ्कुशधरं शूलचक्रोत्पलशरायुधम्।
शङ्खं खट्वाङ्गमुद्गर्य विघ्नराजं नमाम्यहम्॥९॥

ॐ क्षिप्रगणपतये नमः।
पाशाङ्कुशं कल्पवल्लीं कामोदकसमन्वितम्।
रत्नकुम्भं च दोर्दण्डे क्षिप्रं प्रसादनं भजे॥१०॥

ॐ हेरम्बगणपतये नमः।
अभयवरदहस्तं पाशदन्ताक्षमालं
सृणिपरशुदधानं मोदकं पुस्तकं च।
पञ्चमुखं त्रिनेत्रं सिंहवाहं प्रसन्नं
कनकरुचिरदेहं हेरम्बं तं नमामि॥११॥

ॐ लक्ष्मीगणपतये नमः।
शुक्लवर्णं समारूढं पद्मद्वयकरं शुभम्।
बिभ्राणं शुककोकं च लक्ष्मीगणपतिं भजे॥१२॥

ॐ महागणपतये नमः।
तृतीयनेत्रं चन्द्रार्धमौलिं ताम्राधरं रुचिम्।
महागणपतिं वन्दे चिन्तामणिलसत्करम्॥१३॥

ॐ विजयगणपतये नमः।
पाशाङ्कुशस्वदन्तामं वहन्तं मूषकध्वजम्।
विजयं सर्वकार्येषु गणनाथं नमाम्यहम्॥१४॥

ॐ नृत्तगणपतये नमः।
पाशाङ्कुशापूपकुठारहस्तं
मुदा नृत्यन्तं प्रमथैः सहैव।
कल्पद्रुमाधः सुरनर्तकं तं
nṛत्यन्तमीशं गणनाथमीडे॥१५॥

ॐ ऊर्ध्वगणपतये नमः।
कल्हारशालिपदमस्तकमोदकानां
वृन्दं दधानमभितो लसदङ्गशोभम्।
देवीं च पद्मकरया ललितां गृहीत्वा
ऊर्ध्वं गणाधिपतिमाश्रय सिद्धिकामः॥१६॥

॥ इति श्री षोडश गणपति स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shrī Ṣoḍaśa Gaṇapati Stotram ||

Om Bālagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Karasthakadalīchūtapanasekṣukamodakam.
Bālasūryaprabhaṁ vande devaṁ Bālagaṇādhipam.||1||

Om Taruṇagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Pāśāṅkuśāpūpamodakaiḥ kapitthajambūphalairyutam.
Bālārkasadṛśachchhāyaṁ Taruṇaṁ Gaṇanāyakam.||2||

Om Bhaktagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Nārikelāmrakadalīguḍapāyasadhāriṇam.
Śarachchandrābhavapuṣaṁ bhaje Bhaktagaṇādhipam.||3||

Om Vīragaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Betālalūlitabhujaṁ śaktiśūlāṅkuśojjvalam.
Khaḍgachakragadāpāṇiṁ Vīraṁ Vighneśvaraṁ bhaje.||4||

Om Śaktigaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Āliṅgya devīmabhito niṣaṇṇāṁ
Parasparāsaktakaṭīniveśam.
Sandhyāruṇaṁ pāśasṛṇīvahantaṁ
Bhayāpahaṁ Śaktigaṇeśamīḍe.||5||

Om Dvijagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Yajñopavītaṁ dadhataṁ daṇḍakākṣamaṇīguṇam.
Pustakaṁ chottamāmbhojaṁ dvijarūpaṁ Gaṇādhipam.||6||

Om Siddhigaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Pakvachūtaphalaṁ tilamodakaṁ cha susaṁyutam.
Ikṣudaṇḍaṁ cha puṣpaṁ cha dadhataṁ Siddhinayakam.||7||

Om Uchchhiṣṭagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Nīlābjadāḍimīvīṇāpūrṇapātradharaṁ prabhum.
Lalantikasamāyuktamuchchhiṣṭagaṇanāyakam.||8||

Om Vighnagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Pāśāṅkuśadharaṁ śūlachakrotpalaśarāyudham.
Śaṅkhaṁ khaṭvāṅgamudgarya Vighnarājaṁ namāmyaham.||9||

Om Kṣipragaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Pāśāṅkuśaṁ kalpavallīṁ kāmodakasamanvitam.
Ratnakumbhaṁ cha dordaṇḍe kṣipraṁ prasādanaṁ bhaje.||10||

Om Herambagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Abhayavaradahastaṁ pāśadantākṣamālaṁ
Sṛṇiparaśudadhānaṁ modakaṁ pustakaṁ cha.
Pañchamukhaṁ trinetraṁ siṁhavāhaṁ prasannaṁ
Kanakaruchiradehhaṁ Herambaṁ taṁ namāmi.||11||

Om Lakṣmīgaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Śuklavarṇaṁ samārūḍhaṁ padmadvayakaraṁ śubham.
Bibhrāṇaṁ śukakokaṁ cha Lakṣmīgaṇapatiṁ bhaje.||12||

Om Mahāgaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Tṛtīyanetraṁ chandrārdhamauliṁ tāmrādharaṁ ruchim.
Mahāgaṇapatiṁ vande chintāmaṇilasatkaram.||13||

Om Vijayagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Pāśāṅkuśasvadantāmaṁ vahantaṁ mūṣakadhvajam.
Vijayaṁ sarvakāryeṣu Gaṇanāthaṁ namāmyaham.||14||

Om Nṛttagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Pāśāṅkuśāpūpakuṭhārahastaṁ
Mudā nṛtyantaṁ pramathaiḥ sahaiva.
Kalpadrumādhaḥ suranartakaṁ taṁ
Nṛtyantamīśaṁ Gaṇanāthamīḍe.||15||

Om Ūrdhvagaṇapataye Namaḥ.
Kalhāraśālipadamastakadmodakānāṁ
Vṛndaṁ dadhānamabhito lasadaṅgaśobham.
Devīṁ cha padmakarayā lalitāṁ gṛhītvā
Ūrdhvaṁ Gaṇādhipatimāśraya siddhikāmaḥ.||16||

|| Iti Shrī Ṣoḍaśa Gaṇapati Stotraṁ sampūrṇam ||
hindiMeaning: षोडश गणपति स्तोत्र में गणेश जी के सोलह दिव्य रूपों का वर्णन है: (1) बालगणपति - केला, आम, कटहल, गन्ना और मोदक हाथ में लिए बालसूर्य जैसी कान्ति वाले। (2) तरुणगणपति - पाश, अंकुश, अपूप, मोदक, कैथ और जामुन फल से युक्त, उदित सूर्य जैसे। (3) भक्तगणपति - नारियल, आम, केला, गुड़ और पायस धारण करने वाले, शरद चन्द्र जैसे शरीर वाले। (4) वीरगणपति - शक्ति, शूल, अंकुश, खड्ग, चक्र और गदा धारी वीर विघ्नेश्वर। (5) शक्तिगणपति - देवी को आलिंगन किये, सन्ध्या जैसे अरुण वर्ण, भय हरने वाले। (6) द्विजगणपति - यज्ञोपवीत, दण्ड, अक्षमाला, पुस्तक और कमल धारी। (7) सिद्धिगणपति - पके आम, तिलमोदक, गन्ना और पुष्प धारण करने वाले। (8-16) इसी प्रकार उच्छिष्ट, विघ्न, क्षिप्र, हेरम्ब, लक्ष्मी, महा, विजय, नृत्त और ऊर्ध्व गणपति के दिव्य स्वरूपों का वर्णन है।
benefits: Describes all 16 divine forms of Ganapati for complete worship
Each form grants specific blessings from courage to wisdom to wealth
Heramba Ganapati form with five faces grants ultimate protection
Siddhi Ganapati grants spiritual accomplishments and success
Vijaya Ganapati ensures victory in all endeavors
Complete recitation equivalent to worshipping all forms of Ganesha
description: Dedicated to Lord Ganesha, the Shodasha Ganapati Stotram (षोडश गणपति स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. This revered text originates from Sritattvanidhi / Mudgala Purana. Devotees recite this prayer to complete recitation equivalent to worshipping all forms of Ganesha and vijaya Ganapati ensures victory in all endeavors. It is also said to describe all 16 divine forms of Ganapati for complete worship. This sacred text is ideally recited on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Ganesh Chaturthi for enhanced blessings. Comprising 16 verses, it can be completed in approximately 10 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Shodasha Ganapati Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Shodasha Ganapati Stotram - 16 forms of Ganesha in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Complete prayer describing all divine aspects of Lord Ganapati.

seoFaqs.question: From which Puranic source does the Shodasha Ganapati Stotram derive?
seoFaqs.answer: The widely known sixteen-name stotram (Ganesha Shodasha Nama Stotram) originates in the twenty-fourth prakarana of the Upasana Khanda of the Ganesha Purana, one of the two principal Upapuranas devoted exclusively to Ganesha (the other being the Mudgala Purana). The text is also referenced in supporting sections of the Skanda Purana and certain Tantra Sara compendia. The Ganesha Purana attribution gives it canonical status within the Ganapatya tradition, while its succinct sixteen-verse format made it adoptable across Smarta, Vaishnava and Shaiva households as a daily Ganesha invocation. (Sources: Hindupad; Bhakthi.in.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the sixteen names of Ganesha enumerated in the stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The sixteen names are: Sumukha (lovely-faced), Ekadanta (single-tusked), Kapila (tawny-coloured), Gajakarnaka (elephant-eared), Lambodara (large-bellied), Vikata (formidable), Vighna-rajendra (king of obstacle-removers), Vinayaka (foremost leader), Dhumraketu (smoke-bannered), Ganadhyaksha (lord of ganas), Phalachandra (one with the moon on the forehead), Gajanana (elephant-faced), Vakratunda (curved-trunk), Shurpakarna (winnow-eared), Heramba (boon-giver), and Skandapurvaja (elder brother of Skanda). Each name encapsulates a distinct iconographic, mythological or functional aspect of Ganesha. The phala-shruti promises that whoever recites these sixteen at three sandhyas is freed from impediment and attains wealth, learning and freedom from fear. (Sources: Vaidika Vignanam; Mylordganesha.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Shodasha differ from the more commonly recited Dwadasha (twelve-name) stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Sankat Nashan Ganesh Stotra from the Narada Purana enumerates twelve names, while the Ganesha Purana's Shodasha lists sixteen. The first twelve overlap substantially: Sumukha, Ekadanta, Kapila, Gajakarnaka, Lambodara, Vikata, Vighnaraja, Ganesha, Dhumraketu, Ganadhyaksha, Phalachandra and Gajanana appear in both. The Shodasha adds four further names (Vakratunda, Shurpakarna, Heramba, Skandapurvaja) to round out the iconographic spectrum. The choice between the two is largely lineage-based: Smarta and Madhva households tend to favour the twelve-name Sankat Nashan, while Ganapatya and certain Marathi-Konkani brahmin families prefer the fuller sixteen-name version. (Sources: omshivashakti Dwadasa Nama; Hindupad.)

seoFaqs.question: Is the sixteen-name Ganesha stotra related to the Shodasha Ganapati of Maharashtra?
seoFaqs.answer: There is a related but distinct concept in Maharashtra of Shodasha Ganapati referring to sixteen specific Ganesha forms enshrined in temples and family altars: Mahaganapati, Bala Ganapati, Taruna Ganapati, Bhakti Ganapati, Veera Ganapati, Shakti Ganapati, Dvija Ganapati, Siddhi Ganapati, Ucchhishta Ganapati, Vighna Ganapati, Kshipra Ganapati, Heramba Ganapati, Lakshmi Ganapati, Maha Ganapati, Vijaya Ganapati and Dhundhi Ganapati. While the Shodasha-Nama Stotram lists sixteen names and the Shodasha Ganapati lists sixteen iconographic forms, the two converge in the symbolism of sixteen as a number of completeness in Ganapatya theology. Some published Stotra anthologies fuse them into a 'Shodasha Ganapati Dhyana Stotram'. (Sources: Bhakthi.in; Celextel.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is sixteen a recurring sacred number in Ganesha worship?
seoFaqs.answer: The number sixteen (shodasha) signifies completeness in Hindu ritual mathematics. The standard formal puja consists of sixteen offerings (shodashopachara): asana, padya, arghya, achamana, snana, vastra, yajnopavita, gandha, pushpa, dhupa, deepa, naivedya, tambula, dakshina, pradakshina and namaskara. Sixteen is also the number of digits (kalas) of the moon when full, the number of phases of cosmic creation, and the count of nityas in the Sri Vidya tradition. By naming Ganesha sixteen times, the stotram performs a verbal shodashopachara, treating each name as a complete offering. This integration of devotional poetry with ritual numerology is a hallmark of the Ganesha Purana's compositional sophistication. (Sources: Vaidika Vignanam; Hindupad.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the recommended procedure for daily recitation of Shodasha Ganapati Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition recommends recitation immediately after the morning bath, seated on a clean asana facing east or north, with a clay or brass Ganesha murti before a single ghee lamp and an offering of twenty-one durva-grass blades. Sankalpa is taken stating the devotee's name, gotra and intent. The stotram is recited three times in succession: once for vighna-nashana (obstacle removal), once for buddhi-vridhi (intellect-growth) and once for sankalpa-siddhi (intention-fulfilment). It can also be chanted before commencing study, at the start of new financial-year accounts, before signing important contracts and on every Sankashti and Vinayaka Chaturthi. The procedure mirrors that of the Sankat Nashan Stotra but with the sixteen-name expansion. (Sources: Hindudevotionalblog; Hindupad.)
